Get Out There and Sell Yourself With Confidence!
Answers to the Questions That All New Copywriters Have
By Bob Bly for the American Writers & Artists Inc.
Introduction
Is it possible to make $100,000 a year as a freelance copywriter? Absolutely!
Eventually, you'll have more business than you can handle -- repeat business from satisfied clients
as well as word-of-mouth business from marketers who have heard great things about you. But at
first, as with any profession, you've got to do more than hang up your shingle and wait for the
phone to ring. You've got to make yourself known to the people out there who desperately need
your services.
I'm not telling you anything you don't already know.
But maybe you're a little unsure of your ability to do that. Maybe you're feeling a little uneasy
because there are so many "unknowns" staring you in the face right now.
Chances are, there are dozens of questions running through your head. Questions that are keeping
you from getting out there and selling yourself with confidence. Questions that all new copywriters
have:
* "How do I go about selling myself through direct mail … or over the phone … or with a display
ad … or on the Web?"
* "How should I approach a company when I know I can write better copy than they're currently
using?"
* "How do I build a portfolio? And what do I do in the meantime when a prospect wants to see
samples of my work?"
* "How much should I charge? What do I need to know about royalties?"
Given time and experience, you'll know all the answers. But why learn the hard way when I can
give you the answers -- right now.
* * *
Q: How do I get confidence in my work -- enough so that I can go out and get clients? I am
still a copywriting student and feel that I have grabbed a tiger by the tail. Afraid to let go and
afraid to hold on.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 18 --

A: I am the least self-confident person you would ever want to meet -- so if I can do it, you can do
it too. Entire books have been written about how to build confidence and self-esteem, but here are
my quick tips:
1. Work very hard to get good at your craft. Be a total student of direct response.
2. Do the best job you can on every assignment. Put twice as much effort into the work as you feel
the client is paying for.
3. Be modest in your promises to the client. Don't tell him you will beat his control; tell him you
will write the strongest package you can that you feel has the best chance of beating the control.
Under-promise and then over-deliver.
4. Find another student or writer who can be your "copy buddy." To get a second opinion, you read
each other's work before you send it to the client. This gives you greater confidence in the copy you
submit.
Q: How do I get past the feeling that I am never going to be able to sell anything?
A: This is a question that involves motivation, fear of success, fear of failure, and procrastination --
and volumes have been written on these issues.
My best advice: Feel the fear and do it anyway. Meaning: It doesn't matter if you are motivated,
positive, or whatever. If you call 10 prospects a day or send out 10 sales letters a day, you will get
work. Period. No matter how you feel and whether or not you are enthusiastic and confident.
Results are generated by activity, not emotions.
Q: Do you recommend specializing or being a generalist?
A: Be a specialist in at least one area but also take on general assignments when they come. You
can specialize in a subject (e.g., health care) or a medium (e.g., direct mail). I do both. I am a
business-to-business marketing specialist, and I also specialize in direct mail.
Q: Some copywriters position themselves strictly as an independent consultant. Others create
a company name for themselves, but are still primarily freelancers. There are even a few who
actually form writing companies with employees. From the viewpoint of a prospective client,
which business model is the most appealing? Do clients prefer working with an independent
rather than a firm?
A: Publishers and other direct marketers want to hire freelance copywriters. Corporate marketing
communications departments use both. I think you shouldn't use the made-up company name like
ABC Communications unless you really are going to offer a full service, rather than be just a
copywriter. Reason: It creates a false expectation on the part of the client as to what he is getting.
Q: Since I am a woman, will I get more copywriting opportunities initially if I (a) use initials,
(b) use an alias that is genderless, or (c) simply use a nom de plume that is a man's name?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 18 --

A: Use your own name. Gender is not an issue in this field.
Q: I have some experience writing copy, but what if I don't have the right experience?
A: There are three types of clients, and the market is divided almost evenly between them: (1)
Those who will only hire copywriters with the "right experience" (e.g., a blue widget manufacturer
who wants a writer who has written about blue widgets), (2) those who don't require an exact match
between your experience and their product but would like you to at least be in the ballpark (e.g., a
blue widget manufacturer who would like to at least see some samples of copy you've written
selling equipment), and (3) those who don't care if you have the right experience as long as you can
write.
As a beginner, you will concentrate on and get mostly hired by (3) clients. Once you have samples
in a particular area from a (3) client, you can approach other companies in the same business who
are (1) and (2) clients. When they ask if you have the "right experience," you can then show them
samples that prove you do.
Q: When nabbing those first few paid assignments, how do I figure out how long it will take
me to do them so I can complete the jobs on deadline? I'm concerned because the spec
assignment I'm working on now in my copywriting course is taking at least twice as many
hours as I thought it would.
A: You'll have no idea how long a given type of assignment will take until you have three or four
under your belt. Therefore, your experience as a student with your spec assignment should be your
guide: Make your best estimate of how long you think it will take, double it, and set your deadline
based on that.
As you gain experience, you will get paid twice as much and the work will take you half the time.
Q: How do I distinguish myself from other copywriters competing for work -- and leave no
doubt in the client's mind that I am the one who can get the results he or she seeks?
A: When you're a beginner, it is often "fit" that makes a client choose you instead of another
copywriter. For instance, if the client is a parenting magazine and you have five young children,
that might sway them to pick you over another copywriter who is childless. Each situation is
different.
Q: How do I make sure I have work waiting for me as soon as I finish an assignment?
A: The simple answer: To maintain a steady flow of income, figure out how much marketing and
self-promotion you need to get the level of work you want, and then do twice that amount. It's
called "keeping the pipeline full." You must generate a steady stream of leads, so when you finish
assignment A, assignment B is ready and waiting.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 18 --

Q: My goal is to build my freelance income enough to create a cash cushion and have several
steady clients on board before jumping into my own practice. Am I being too cautious?
A: You are being smart. If you dive in without a cash cushion, you will be pressured to start getting
a lot of jobs faster, and you may have to give up if you can't meet your monthly nut each month.
Q: Will all of my clients already be familiar with the benefits of direct-response marketing?
A: You will have clients who are experienced direct marketers with businesses that are direct-
response driven. But you will also have less-knowledgeable clients who may use direct mail to
generate leads but are not in the direct-marketing business per se.
Experienced direct marketers primarily want you to write copy for direct-mail packages. They
know how to market and are not looking for advice from you. They just need copy that will beat
their control.
The less-knowledgeable clients may need copy, may need advice, and may want both. If they need
advice, you should charge them separately for a consultation. Hourly rates for direct-marketing
consultants are usually in the $100 to $400 range, though a few charge more. The American
Consultants League has a great course on how to make money as a consultant. For information
about it, click on www.americanconsultantsleague.com.
Q: Is it worth it to try to talk a business that has never used DM before into using it?
A: It is almost never worth your time. It's best to offer to write materials that they recognize they
need and are already using. Do not try to convince a client to do something they don't do in the
hopes you can write it for them.
Q: Where's the work?
A: There are multiple markets. I can name just three off the top of my head: (a) newsletter
publishers (big users of direct mail), (b) magazine publishers, (c) software companies. Contact the
marketing director at software companies; the circulation director for newsletters and magazines.
There are about a dozen big direct mailers, including Agora, 21st Century, KCI, Boardroom,
Phillips, Rodale, Weiss Research, and Lombardi. You can find a complete listing in Who's Mailing
What, a directory sold by Target Market magazine.
Outside of publishing, the biggest money for copywriters is in medical and health care, financial
services, and annual reports. Plus, though the boom in technology stocks has ended, technology
itself is still going strong -- and there is a ton of work out there with software, IT, and
semiconductor businesses.
Q: What's the most lucrative type of copywriting work to go after?
A: I like a mix, but you can make $100K doing either long-form or short-form work.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 18 --

Say you charge $1,000 for a short lead-generating letter and each letter takes one full day. You do
two a week. You make $100K a year at that rate. You spend two more days each week marketing,
dealing with clients and prospects, running your business. You are making $100K a year working
four days a week.
If you do big packages, say you get $8,000 a package. It takes two full weeks to do a package.
Royalties aside, you make $96,000 on flat fees alone just by doing one package a month.
Q: How do I put together a client contract? I've heard that, for my protection, I have to make
sure to include an indemnification clause.
A: This is a legal issue, and so you have to consult a lawyer. Here is what my agreements say -- but
I do not claim that it gives you any protection:
"Although Bob makes every effort to make your copy comply with the law, he is not an attorney.
Therefore, it is your responsibility to submit all copy for legal review. You are also responsible for
final proofreading of all the copy."
Q: What kind of marketing pieces do I need for my services?
A: You need at least something to send to people who want more information. This can be as
simple as a one-page bio or small brochure, or as complex as a multi-part information kit.
When you are experienced, your promotional package shows what you have done, who you have
done it for, and the results achieved. But when you are starting out, your promotional package says
what you can and will do, who you will do it for, and the advantages and benefits of doing business
with you.
When I started out, I had a simple tri-fold brochure, and that was it. Then, as I got samples, a client
list, and testimonials, I gradually added them to the package I was sending out.
Q: I'm concerned about the cost of marketing myself. If I send out 400 letters, it will cost $148
in postage alone. I really need to be making money now -- maybe not 100 grand a year, but
enough to pay off my debts. What would you advise?
A: Getting into freelance copywriting is about the least capital-intensive business I can think of.
(To open something like a McDonald's franchise, for example, it would cost $100,000 or more.)
But you can’t expect to spend nothing. If you set your first-year income goal at just $50,000, figure
you have to spend 5% on marketing -- which would come to $2,500. You have to spend something
to make money. If you are not willing to spend $148 on a mailing, you won't succeed.
Q: Given my lack of experience, how do I persuade companies to hire me?
A: Some companies are looking to work only with established pros. But others prefer to work with
up-and-coming copywriters, usually because they think they'll be more flexible and more
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 18 --

reasonably priced. Also, many of the big-name copywriters are booked months in advance, so their
clients will call on you and other less-experienced copywriters to handle the assignments their
regular writers cannot take on.
Q: What do you think of offering to rewrite a prospect's direct-mail package on spec? Is that
a good way to get people to give me a chance?
A: I am not sure I would aggressively offer to do work on spec, because that is precisely what
makes you look like a beginner. (Established, experienced writers do not work on spec.) Instead,
charge them, but guarantee their satisfaction: "If you are not 100% satisfied, I will rewrite the letter
at my expense. You don't pay me a cent until you have a letter you are pleased with and proud of
and ready to mail."
Q: How do I land jobs for big companies that can give me regular work? I'm not interested in
working for small, local companies and would like to position myself as a freelancer for the
bigger companies like Rodale, Agora, Phillips, and Boardroom.
A: Start with whoever will hire you and write mailings that are profitable for them. Then, take your
successful mailings to big companies to show them what you can do. One will give you a shot. If
that mailing is successful, they will give you another. If you regularly produce winners for them,
they will hire you on a regular basis.
Q: As companies downsize, they're releasing countless personnel who were responsible for
both in-house and external communications. I'm wondering how I, as a freelance copywriter,
might link up with these companies. As a "copywriter on call," I would be available, but only
when they need me. This would be an efficient, cost-saving approach for them -- but I'm not
sure most companies even know freelance copywriters exist.
A: Best method. Send a letter letting them know of your services and availability. (You can get a
list of corporate marketing managers from Creative Access at 312-440-1140.) In your letter, offer
to send additional information, such as samples of your work and a bio.
When they respond, follow up with a phone call. Ask if they have work and use freelancers from
time to time (most do). Then, ask point-blank what it would take for you to be considered to handle
a project for them. If they are hesitant, suggest a small trial project -- and guarantee that if they are
not satisfied with your work, there will be no charge.
Q: While building a side business as a freelance copywriter, I am working at an ad agency as
my 9-5 job. I have never stolen a client from my agency, but if I build my own client list, is
that an indirect theft? Am I professionally obligated to always steer clients to the agency that
employs me?
A: Tricky situation. Best strategy is to target accounts that don't want an agency but just want copy.
Fortunately, most large direct marketers (not all) prefer freelance copywriters to ad agencies.
Q: How do I go about contacting potential clients?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 18 --

A: Direct mail has always been very effective. Response rates are not as great as they once were,
mainly because of the economy -- but that will turn around. And you can still get good results with
it. Small classified ads in business, advertising, and marketing magazines and newsletters can work,
too, though the volume they bring is modest. And many freelancers use e-mail marketing and cold
calling with success.
Q: Do you think cold calling is more effective right now than direct mail?
A: I prefer direct mail, but have done well with both. Reasons I prefer direct mail: (a) I hate to cold
call, (b) cold calling takes time, (c) direct-mail results are equal or better.
Test to see which works best for you.
Q: I've been sending direct-mail solicitations to advertising and marketing firms with limited
success. I know that I have to present these prospects with something they can use to profit
their businesses. What do they need? Proven talent? Inexpensive services?
A: Every client is different. Some will hire you because they believe you are the best. Other will
hire you because you can do it by Tuesday. Do enough marketing and you will come in contact
with enough clients for whom you are a good "fit" and you'll make a very comfortable living. Best
way to find out what a client wants and values: Ask him.
Q: You say different clients have different needs. If that's the case, how can a direct-mail
letter work if I send the same one to hundreds of prospective clients?
A: It won't. But even if 1% of those who get your letter like you and hire you, you will have a
successful business. Look at the economics: It costs less than $100 to send 100 letters. If one
person hires you for just one small $1,000 project, your ROI (return on investment) is 10:1.
You can't be all things to all people. The trick is to test different letters until you find one that pulls
in more leads than your current letter.
Q: The conclusion of my sales letter directs the prospect to e-mail me or visit me on the Web
for more information. Is this method just as effective as providing a reply card?
A: No. You need a reply card. Without it, your response will be cut in half.
Q: If the headline on my solicitation letter talks about how bad the economy is, will it attract
only clients who care about costs?
A: No, it's going to attract clients who agree with you that the economy is bad and perceive they
need to do better marketing to survive.
Q: Should I use attention-getting teaser copy on the envelopes that I'm sending to prospects?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 18 --

A: No. Just use a plain envelope with your name and return address printed on the outside in plain
type. Do not use a logo or the word "copywriter" on the outer envelope.
Q: How can I best identify new potential clients so I can reach them through a combination of
direct mail and follow-up phone calls?
A: The key is to pick a narrow target market (e.g., marketing directors of book publishers). For
virtually any market you can define, there are lists available you can use for prospecting.
When I started, I got a list of ad agencies and sent 500 letters to the creative directors by name,
offering to write whatever they needed. I got 35 replies and was in business!
Q: Suppose I want a specific list of companies and contact names -- maybe companies that
frequently use direct mail. Is there someone who can provide information like this at an
affordable cost?
A: If you're planning a substantial mailing (at least 5,000 pieces), call a mailing-list broker such as
Edith Roman Associates (800-223-2194). Tell them the market you want to reach, and they will
find lists that target those prospects.
For smaller mailings, try to ferret out groups and associations and rent their membership directories
or lists directly. You have to look around.
Q: I belong to a trade association. As a member, I can view the entire membership roster
online. There is a warning that the list is "not to be used for commercial purposes." Does that
mean I can't send a prospecting letter to each name on the roster?
A: Yes, that's what it means. Even though you're a member, you'll have to pay for the list in order
to mail to it.
Q: I sent out my first 75 direct-mail solicitations a few weeks ago, and I am getting letters
back that are undeliverable -- address unknown. There are five so far. Is this normal?
A: Most mailing-list brokers guarantee 93% or better deliverability, and you are at 94.6% -- within
the acceptable norm.
Q: I've solicited lots of companies that looked at my work and said they may have work for
me in the future -- but I never heard from them again. How do I keep in touch without feeling
that I'm being a pest?
A: There are three good ways to follow up with prospects and clients so they don't forget about you
when they have an assignment:
1. Use a contact manager like Telemagic or Act to periodically follow up with them on the phone.
2. Create an information-packed monthly e-zine that you e-mail to them.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 18 --

3. Clip and send articles that might be of interest to them, along with a personal note or your
business card.
Q: I am feeling a little down about the job I am doing. I handed in the concept and first
section on Tuesday or Wednesday but haven't heard back from the client, despite sending
two e-mails.
A: Clients respond according to their timetable, not yours. It is not unusual for a client to demand a
job in a rush and then sit on it for weeks. Do not read anything into it. And do not get discouraged.
This is part of the job.
Q: I am considering implementing a cold-call program to target specific markets. Instead of
making these cold calls myself, I'm going to hire a small tele-selling firm. What do I need to
know before I do this?
A: This is a good idea. To plan the campaign, you will need a script or call guidelines. Then, test
the tele-selling firm on a small sample of names -- 100 would be great. Evaluate progress after the
first 100 calls, then continue as is or redo the script based on the response.
In most instances, the tele-selling company will not be able to close on the phone. Here is the
hierarchy of offers they should be prepared to make:
Offer No. 1. If the prospect is really interested and has either an immediate job or an ongoing need,
the tele-selling company should arrange for you to talk with them over the phone.
Offer No. 2. If the prospect has no immediate need but might have a future need, the tele-selling
firm should offer to send your information kit with samples tailored to the prospect's copywriting
needs and industry. They should then follow up in a week or two to see if the prospect can be
moved to Offer No. 1. If not, the prospect should be scheduled to receive regular follow-ups at 3-
month intervals (approximately) with the goal of moving them to Offer No. 1.
Offer No. 3. If the prospect is not biting on Offer No. 2, the tele-selling firm should sweeten the pot
by offering to also send a valuable free report or some other information premium that you create
(i.e., "10 Ways to Improve Readership and Response to Your Newsletter").
Q: On a cold call, what do I say to the screener?
A: Treat screeners with respect and, if possible, engage them as your allies (if they seem receptive
and friendly) -- but do not involve them in discussions about copywriting, marketing, or the
services you offer. Simply ask, "Is [name] the person in charge of marketing?" If the answer is
"yes," ask to be connected. If the answer is "no," ask who you should speak with.
You'll have a better chance of reaching decision makers directly if you make your calls when they
are likely to be in the office but not busy -- before 9 a.m., after 5 p.m., or during lunch.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 18 --

Q: When I do get in touch with a decision maker -- as the result of a cold call or a lead
generated by a direct-mail letter -- what questions should I ask?
A: The only three questions you should ask:
1. "Are you the person in charge of marketing?"
2. "Do you use freelance copywriters?"
3. "What would it take to be considered for a copywriting assignment from you?"
After you ask the third question, the prospect will tell you what he wants to know about you. After
that, he will reveal his needs, concerns, and wants.
Don't lecture the prospect on direct marketing or your services. Let him ask the questions -- and
then respond by showing him how you can solve his problems.
Q: How do I generate leads for my copywriting services using small ads?
A: Try small display or classified ads in publications that service local and regional businesses.
You can test whether a newspaper or magazine will generate leads with a single insertion. This will
usually cost less than $200.
I had my best luck with small classified ads in industry publications such as Adweek and DM
News. Keep in mind that classified ad space is charged by the word, so the trick is to get your
message across in the fewest possible words.
Q: Would it be possible to launch and operate a successful copywriting business online? In
other words, by attracting potential clients to a Website offering my services?
A: Online marketing has been proven effective for many different types of service businesses,
including freelance copywriting. The primary concept is that it works best when you e-mail to
people who already know you. Therefore, successful online marketers build their "house file" or "e-
list" (lists of prospects and their e-mail addresses) and then sell their products/services to those
people via e-mail marketing.
Here's how you do it:
1. Build a Website that positions you as an expert or guru in your field (e.g., direct-mail
copywriting). This is the "base of operations" for your online marketing campaign.
2. Your Website should include, at minimum, a home page, your bio, and a page with brief
descriptions of your services (writing catalogs, display ads, direct mail packages, Websites). Later,
you should add (a) an "Articles Page," where you post articles you have written on marketing that
visitors can read and download for free, and (b) an online Portfolio, where you post samples of
your work (making sure you get permission from your clients first). See www.bly.com for a model.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 18 --

3. Write a short special report on your area of expertise (e.g., "10 Secrets of Writing Powerful
Fundraising Copy") and make this available to people who visit your site. Before they can
download it, they have to register and give you their e-mail address (and any other information you
want to capture).
Consider also offering a monthly online newsletter or "e-zine" to people who visit your site -- a free
subscription if they register and give you their e-mail address.
The more "content" (useful information) on your site, the better. More people will be attracted to it
and they will spend more time there. They will also tell others about it.
4. The model is to drive traffic to your site and then get people to sign up for either your free report
or your free e-zine. Once they register, you have their e-mail addresses and can now market your
services to them via e-mail as often as you like -- at no extra cost to you.
5. The bulk of your online leads will come from repeat e-mail marketing to this "house" e-list of
prospects. Therefore, your goal is to build a large e-list of qualified prospects as quickly and
inexpensively as you can.
6. There are a number of online marketing options that can drive traffic to your site. These include:
free publicity; e-mail marketing; banner advertising; co-registrations; affiliate marketing; search-
engine optimization; direct mail; and e-zine advertising.
The key to success is to try a lot of different tactics in small and inexpensive tests, throw out the
ones that don't work, and do more of the ones that are effective.
A Word of Caution: Writing e-mail copy that is effective and avoids spam is a big topic that you
could write a book on -- and, in fact, I have. Check out my book, The Online Copywriter's
Handbook, and go to www.bly.com and click on "Articles" to read some free information on the
topic.
Q: I'm an 18-year copywriting veteran and am just now getting around to setting up a
Website. Problem is, I've been really bad about getting testimonials from people I've worked
with -- although I've worked with some of the world's most prestigious companies. I can't go
back to people I haven't worked with in over a year, so I'm gathering what I can. Right now, I
have only about 10 testimonials. Should I wait to get more before posting them? Won't it look
bad to have just 10 after 18 years in business?
A: Post them immediately. Ten is plenty. After all, nowhere on your site do you say these are the
only testimonials you have, right? The reader will assume she is looking at a selective sampling.
By the way, don't forget to get releases for any testimonials you use -- on your Website or any other
promotional material.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 18 --

Q: I've received a DM letter from a national organization and I know I can do much better.
How do I approach them?
A: Do not contact direct mailers, tell them their mailings stink, and say that you can do much
better. This doesn't work for several reasons.
To begin with, you do not know that the mailing is not working. If you assume that it is not, but it
is indeed working well, you lose credibility. Also, the person you are approaching is probably the
one responsible for it -- so why start off the relationship by insulting her?
A better approach is to call or write the contact person, tell her you notice they use direct mail, and
ask if she would like to see samples of your work. Then you ask what it would take for you to be
considered to write a mailing for them.
Sometimes, the contact person is the one who signed the letter. Sometimes, it's the marketing
director. Call the company (their contact information is usually in the mailing package or on their
Website) and ask for the person in charge of marketing.
Once you know a client and have been dealing with them for a while, it's OK to drop them a line
and say, "Saw your XYZ mailing. How's it working?" I just got a $5,000 assignment to write an
insert this way.
Q: Is it a good idea to promote my freelance copywriting business by submitting articles to
targeted newspapers in my area?
A: This is a very good way to find clients. Except for repeat business and referrals, more of my
business comes from writing articles and books than from any other source.
Contact the editor of the business section of the newspaper by sending him a one-page "query
letter" describing the article you want to write. (You can find sample query letters in my book Write
More Sell More.) You might also target business journals and marketing-oriented trade journals
read by your prospects. Editors usually prefer a strong letter suggesting one specific article, but, at
times, a more general letter proposing a range of topics can work.
Suggest a length based on the length of other articles in the same publication on similar topics. For
instance, if you want to write a column and other columns are 500 words, suggest a length of 500
words for the article you plan to submit.
Follow up in a few weeks with a phone call to see whether he received your letter, read it, and has
any interest. If he gives you the go-ahead, ask if a deadline of three weeks is OK. If he needs it
sooner (unusual) or has more time (typical), he will tell you.
Q: Prospective clients always want to see samples, a catch-22 for us newbies with shallow
portfolios. What should we do?
A: There are two answers. I would do both.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 18 --

Answer No. 1. Create a portfolio. Here are a few suggestions:
* Take existing ads or promos, rewrite them to make them better, and use them as samples.
* Do pro bono work for non-profit organizations.
* Ask friends, relatives, associates, neighbors, and colleagues if you can do a mailer for
their business free, in exchange for printed samples, a testimonial (if it works), and using
them as a reference.
* Offer your services to small ad agencies. Make sure you get samples of everything you
do.
* Target local small businesses offering direct mail that can bring them new leads and sales.
Work on spec if you have to, but get the samples.
* Use clean copies of completed copywriting course assignments if they are strong.
It's also a good idea to do a nice little write-up on yourself and include that. (For a sample, go to
www.bly.com and click on my bio.)
Answer No. 2. Tell the prospect, "Send me your current piece. I will do a free critique. If you like it,
hire me to write a new promo. Only pay for it if it beats your control. If you do not want to hire me,
you got a critique for nothing." That's a good deal for them with minimal risk.
Q: I'm pulling together my portfolio samples -- but I don't know what to do about the names,
addresses, and phone numbers of the people I've written for. Do I cross them out to protect
their identity?
A: I photocopy and send the samples exactly as they were printed.
Q: The only direct-response samples I have were done for high-tech, business-to-business,
very high-dollar stuff that most of the people I have shown them to can't understand
(although they pulled very well). What do I do?
A: There are tons of business-to-business and high-tech direct marketers and a huge demand for
sales letters, DM packages, and e-mail marketing campaigns to generate leads for their products.
Those folks will respond very well to your samples and success stories.
Q: How do I market my creative skills (both copy and art) from my ad agency days to direct-
response clients?
A: Your best bet: Target direct marketers in industries and product areas similar to those you
worked on as an ad-agency creative. You can show them a combination of direct-marketing
samples you have created, as well as your "real" stuff in areas related to theirs.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 18 --

Any industry in which you already have experience or product knowledge is where you will have
an edge over other copywriters. For example, if you have written ad copy for Ford, approach Ford
dealers about doing their dealer direct-mail promotions.
Q: I am a writer and a graphic designer, and would like to find clients for which I could do
both. Any suggestions?
A: Two choices:
1. Promote yourself as a freelance copywriter. When clients hire you to write their copy, offer to do
the layout for an additional fee.
2. Promote yourself as a small ad agency or full-service marketing communications firm that does
both copy and graphics.
Q: I have published 50 articles and two books for the Christian market. Given my experience,
what approach should I take to selling myself as a copywriter?
A: There is a lot of business for direct-response copywriters in the Christian market. Approach
Christian magazines, book publishers, and book clubs. They prefer to work with writers (like you)
who are experienced in this market. Once you have a few great clients and samples in Christian
books and magazines, you can use them to market your services to other publishers. If your
Christian work is good, mainstream publishers will be glad to hire you.
Q: I have an engineering degree. I also have a decade of experience working as an engineer
(primarily in software) and have done some graduate work in physics as well. Now I'm
switching careers to copywriting and need to understand how to best leverage this
background.
A: You want to position yourself as a "business-to-business/high-tech copywriter," which means
you will specialize in writing about technology and technology-related products (software,
semiconductors, computers, test equipment, etc.).
Your clients will be marketing directors at high-tech and industrial companies. They need direct
mail, e-mail marketing, Web copy, white papers, brochures, ads, and other marketing materials.
Best way to reach them: Join the Business Marketing Association (www.marketing.org), do a
mailing to their membership list, and attend local meetings if they have a chapter near you.
Q: I am currently employed as a writer at a computer software company, where I create both
marketing materials and technical publications (user guides, on-screen help, etc.). How can I
use my existing skills set to jump-start my freelance copywriting career?
A: Gather multiple samples of everything you write. Approach software and other technology
companies (not directly competitive with your employer) as a freelance copywriter specializing in
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 18 --

high-tech. When they ask about experience, offer to send a package of samples -- and then send
copies of things you have written for your current boss.
Q: How do I charge for my services?
A: You can charge by the word, by the hour, or by the project. Figure 50 cents to $1 per word, $50
to $200 per hour. Copywriters who service small local clients charge near the low end; those who
work with major national accounts can charge the high end.
Most freelance direct-response copywriters charge by the project. My personal fee schedule reads
as follows:
Print ad: $750-$3,500
Sales letter: $2,000-$5,000+
Direct-mail package, lead generation: $3,000-$5,500
Direct-mail package, mail-order: $3,000-$15,000
Renewal series: $1,500/letter
Blanket renewal insert: $2,000-$2,500
Magalog: $10,000-$17,000
Self-mailer: $1,500-$3,500
Postcard or double postcard: $950-$1,500
Brochure: $750/page
Data sheet: $750/page
Catalog: $750/page
Newsletter: $750/page
Feature story: $1.50/word
White paper: $1.50/word
Press release (1-2 pages): $750-$850
Audiovisual or multimedia script: $250/minute
Website, home page: $1,500
Website, additional pages: $750/page
E-mail promo: $2,000-$2,500
E-zine ad: $750
Landing page: $950-$2,500
Micro-site: $5,000-$7,500
Telemarketing script: $2,000-$2,500
Copy critique: $300-$1,000
My copywriting fee includes up to three concepts the client can choose from, as well as turning one
of those concepts into a full package.
Q: My client just asked for one hour's work from me, to make a document more persuasive.
What do you charge for an hour of your time?
A: I charge $300 an hour for consulting. For copywriting in situations such as yours, I have a
special editing fee of $150 an hour.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 18 --

Q: What would be a reasonable price range for an electronic (e-mail) sales letter?
A: I generally don't give specific price suggestions for this reason: I don't want to be blamed if you
lose a job because the price you quote is too high. Fees are all over the lot, especially for e-mail
copy -- and what one client will pay without blinking will blow another client away for the same
job.
Having said that, I see fees for a single e-mail marketing message ranging from $500 to $2,500,
with many clients looking to pay around $1,000 to $1,500 per e-mail.
Q: I'm quoting on a Website job for a client who also wants a new tag line. What should I
charge for that?
A: Tag lines can run $500 to $2,500 from a copywriter; corporate brand consulting firms charge
$50,000 for essentially the same work.
You can't really quote on a Website until the client has made a site map or outline that shows you
how many pages are on it. Then, you can quote per page, anywhere from $500 to $1,500 per page
on the site.
Q: I just got off of the phone with a gentleman who asked me to submit a bid for a project.
Apparently another copywriter had better samples (although he said my price was
competitive). It seems to me that bidding for jobs is unprofessional -- too much like an
auction. What do you think?
A: I have mixed feelings. You do feel somewhat "used" -- but the fact is, people get multiple bids
for services all the time. (I just got three quotes to pave my driveway.) So there is no harm in giving
a bid as long as the process does not take a lot of your time.
Q: Prospects in the software industry are saying my prices are out of their range? Am I
overpriced?
A: If everyone in an industry says they can't afford you, yes. You either have to lower your pricing
or find another market where clients can pay the fees you feel you have to get.
Q: If you have to meet with a client, do you charge from the time you leave your office until
the time you return -- or do you charge only for the meeting itself and discount travel time?
A: Standard practice is not to charge for travel time within an hour of your office.
Many copywriters will do a first meeting with a potential client at no charge in hopes of getting
business from them. I do not -- but, as a beginner, I did.
Q: How do you handle "kill fees"?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 18 --

A: My agreement says: "If you cancel or put the project on hold once I have begun work on it, a
kill fee may be applied. Let me know if you'd like a copy of our kill fee schedule."
About 10% of clients ask to see the kill fee schedule before going forward, and I send it to them;
the others don't. However, because I offered it to them in the agreement that they signed, I am
protected if they want to kill the project or put it on hold for any reason.
Q: How do you raise your rates?
A: If you agree to a low price for the first job with a new client, make very clear that it is a one-
time-only offer, a get-acquainted, try-me-out price. Tell the client up front, in no uncertain terms,
that if he likes your copy and it works for him, he will have to pay your regular rate on subsequent
jobs.
If you have a client to whom you sold yourself too low and did not give the above warning, it is
difficult but not impossible to raise your rates. Here's how to do it.
Let's say you once did a job for $500 for this client and you now charge other clients $1,000 for the
same thing. Next time the client calls, say, "I have raised my rates. You paid $500. A letter (or
whatever) is now $1,000. But for you, I will offer a discounted rate for the rest of this year of $750.
Starting January 1 of next year, it will become the full $1,000."
Clients understand that prices go up and raising them only halfway softens the blow. They may
even be grateful for the break you are giving them at $750, which is still 25% below your list price.
Q: I don't know much about royalties. In fact, I am receiving royalties from only one direct-
mail package I wrote, and that fee structure was initiated by the client, not me. What can you
tell me?
A: Usually, only major direct marketers pay royalties.
Royalties are often a "mailing fee" of 1 to 4 cents for every package mailed after the initial test. So
if the rollout is 1 million packages mailed and your mailing fee is 2 cents, you get a royalty of
$20,000.
Some people pay a flat bonus if your letter becomes the control, often double the price you charged
for it. For example, if your $5,000 letter becomes the control, you get an additional $5,000 as a
bonus.
Very few pay a royalty based on sales or profits. And if they do, you have to be careful. Without a
system in place to do so, it's very easy for a client to "forget" to track sales and royalties and just
not pay them.
Q: What is the single most important insight you've discovered in your 20-year career as a
copywriter?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 18 --

A: The copywriter who makes money for her clients -- whether by beating controls, creating
winning offers, or suggesting profitable marketing strategy -- can write her own ticket.

Delray Beach, FL 33483

-- 18 of 18 --
